comprehensive_summary:
Eugene Ionesco (Eugen Ionescu) is a Romanian-French absurdist playwright best known for The Bald Soprano, Rhinoceros, The Chairs, The Lesson, and Exit the King. He is a central figure in the Theatre of the Absurd and created the recurring character Berenger, who appears across multiple plays.

Rhinoceros is his most famous play. In it, Berenger is the only character who refuses to transform into the title animal. His friend Jean's skin turns green as he transforms. The typist Daisy and Dudard also transform, and Daisy leaves Berenger. A logician uses a syllogism to prove a dog is a cat. A cat is trampled. Berenger shouts 'I'm not capitulating!' at the end. The play satirizes French behavior under German occupation; the green skin represents the green uniforms of the Iron Guard and Orpo. The Israeli word for getting swayed by nationalistic fervor was coined from the play's title.

The Bald Soprano is Ionesco's first play, an 'anti-play' set in an 'English interior, with English armchairs.' Everything in the opening is described as 'English' seventeen times. The Smiths and the Martins engage in surreal conversation after the Fire Chief mentions the title character. Mrs. Smith responds that the bald soprano 'always wears her hair the same way.' The maid Mary claims her real name is Sherlock Holmes and recites a poem about objects catching fire. Bobby Watson's death is discussed -- every member of his family is named Bobby Watson. The Fire Chief settles the 'doorbell debate' and tells an experimental fable. Ionesco was inspired to write it after reading an English language primer (Assimil), whose textbook opens with 'My tailor is rich.' Originally directed by Nicholas Bataille. The Theatre de la Huchette has permanently staged it and The Lesson for 70 years.

The Chairs is a 'tragic farce' in which the Old Man and Old Woman arrange chairs for invisible guests on their island. The Old Man introduces his wife as Semiramis and greets the Lady, the Photo-engraver, and the Belle. The deaf-mute Orator writes 'ANGELFOOD' on a chalkboard. An invisible Emperor arrives. The Old Man and Old Woman throw themselves out of a window. The Old Man imitates the month of February and addresses 'all that is left from humanity' as 'leftovers.'

The Lesson is a one-act play in which 'the Pupil' impresses her teacher by naming all four seasons and can add and multiply but not subtract. The maid warns 'Philology leads to calamity!' The Professor ignores her toothache and stabs her to death, restarting the cycle with the next pupil. The Professor wears an armband with a swastika.

Exit the King features Berenger as a 400-year-old king who confronts his impending death. His two wives, Marie and Marguerite, react differently -- Marguerite tells him 'You're going to die at the end of the show' while Marie tries to prevent him from learning. He is credited with inventing the tractor, setting the Thames on fire, and writing all of Shakespeare's plays.

The Killer features Berenger in a 'radiant city' where a murderer drowns his victims. The killer uses a 'picture of a colonel' to lure Dany to her death. The killer does almost nothing except chuckle and shrug.

Other works include Amedee, or How to Get Rid of It (about an ever-growing corpse), The New Tenant (a man trapped under ever-increasing furniture), A Stroll in the Air (Berenger can fly; inspired by Ionesco's childhood experience of floating in a whitewashed village), Jack, or Obedience and The Future is in Eggs (Jack rejects a girl for having two noses but marries Roberta; ends with egg-hatching), Macbett (inspired by Macbeth, featuring a lemonade seller and butterfly catcher), Improvisation, or The Shepherd's Chameleon (playwright Ionesco is bullied by three versions of Bartholomeus), and the critical essays Nu. In 1958, Kenneth Tynan touched off the 'London Controversy' with a negative review. Ionesco was a member of the College de Pataphysique alongside Duchamp and the Marx Brothers.

works.name: Rhinoceros
works.description: Rhinoceros follows Berenger as the only character who refuses to transform into the title animal. Jean's skin turns green. Daisy leaves Berenger. A logician proves a dog is a cat. Berenger shouts 'I'm not capitulating!' The play satirizes French collaboration under occupation.

clues.clue: Berenger is the only character who refuses to transform into the title animal; shouts 'I'm not capitulating!'
quotes: every character except for him turns into the title animal
Berenger's refusal to transform

clues.clue: Jean's skin turns green during his transformation; the typist Daisy leaves Berenger
quotes: Jean's skin turns green and the typist Daisy leaves Berenger
Dudard and Daisy also transform

clues.clue: A logician uses a syllogism to prove a dog is a cat; a cat is trampled
quotes: A logician uses a syllogism to prove that a dog is a cat
a cat is trampled, the Logician is identified by his boater hat

clues.clue: Satirizes French behavior under German occupation; green skin represents Iron Guard and Orpo uniforms
quotes: intended as a satire of French behavior under German occupation. The green skin of the title creatures were meant to represent the green uniforms of the Iron Guard and Orpo.

works.name: The Bald Soprano
works.description: The Bald Soprano is Ionesco's first play, an 'anti-play' set in an 'English interior' where everything is described as 'English' 17 times. The Smiths and Martins engage in surreal conversation. The Fire Chief mentions the title character, who 'always wears her hair the same way.' The maid Mary claims to be Sherlock Holmes. Inspired by an English primer (Assimil).

clues.clue: Anti-play set in 'English interior, with English armchairs'; the Smiths and Martins engage in surreal conversation
quotes: set in an 'English interior, with English armchairs,' the Smiths and the Martins engage in surreal conversation
a play about the breakdown of language between the Martins and the Smiths

clues.clue: Fire Chief mentions the title character; Mrs. Smith says she 'always wears her hair the same way'; settles the 'doorbell debate'
quotes: after the Fire Chief mentions the title character
the title character 'always wears her hair the same way'

clues.clue: Maid Mary claims her real name is Sherlock Holmes; recites poem about objects catching fire
quotes: this character, who claims her real name is Sherlock Holmes
recites a poem beginning with the line 'the polypoids were burning in the wood'

clues.clue: Bobby Watson's death discussed; every family member named Bobby Watson
quotes: This character whose death notice in the newspaper does not surprise Mr. Smith, even though this man died two years ago.

clues.clue: Inspired by English language primer (Assimil); originally directed by Nicholas Bataille; Theatre de la Huchette permanently stages it
quotes: turned the sentences in an English textbook into a play
Ionesco was inspired to write The Bald Soprano by his experience learning English from materials provided by Assimil

clues.clue: Opening uses the adjective 'English' 17 times; characters repeat 'How curious, how bizarre, what a coincidence!'
quotes: The opening paragraph describing the setting uses this adjective 17 times.
characters who repeat lines like 'How curious, how bizarre, what a coincidence!'

works.name: The Chairs
works.description: The Chairs is a 'tragic farce' in which the Old Man and Old Woman arrange chairs for invisible guests. The deaf-mute Orator writes 'ANGELFOOD' on a chalkboard. An invisible Emperor arrives. The couple throws themselves out of a window. The Old Man imitates February.

clues.clue: Old Man and Old Woman arrange chairs for invisible guests on their island; throw themselves out of a window at the end
quotes: the Old Man and Old Woman, arrange a play's title objects for invisible guests
two elderly characters to jump out of a window

clues.clue: Deaf-mute Orator writes 'ANGELFOOD' on a chalkboard; invisible Emperor arrives
quotes: the deaf-mute Orator writes 'ANGELFOOD' on a chalkboard before the Emperor arrives
the Old Man and the Old Woman prepare for the arrival of an orator

clues.clue: Old Man introduces wife as Semiramis; imitates the month of February; addresses 'leftovers' that 'can make a very good soup'
quotes: the Old Man imitates the month of February
addresses 'all that is left from humanity' as 'leftovers'

works.name: The Lesson

clues.clue: The Pupil names all four seasons, can add and multiply but not subtract; the Professor stabs her, restarting the cycle

clues.clue: Maid warns 'Philology leads to calamity!'; Professor ignores the Pupil's toothache
quotes: a maid warns a professor that 'Philology leads to calamity!'
the teacher ignores her toothache and stabs her to death

works.name: Exit the King
works.description: Exit the King features Berenger as a 400-year-old king confronting death. His wives Marie and Marguerite react differently. He is credited with inventing the tractor and writing all of Shakespeare's plays.

one of these people is told, 'You're going to die at the end of the show,' by one of his two wives

works.name: Other Works
works.description: The Killer features Berenger in a 'radiant city' where a murderer lures victims with a 'picture of a colonel.' Amedee features an ever-growing corpse. The New Tenant has proliferating furniture. A Stroll in the Air has Berenger flying. Jack, or Obedience features Roberta with two noses.

clues.clue: The Killer: Berenger in the 'radiant city'; murderer uses 'picture of a colonel' to lure Dany; the killer just chuckles and shrugs
quotes: a murderer who drowns his victims haunts a 'radiant city'
uses a 'picture of a colonel' to lure Dany to her death

clues.clue: Amedee: an ever-growing corpse in the living room; based on short story 'Oriflamme'; feet burst through a door
quotes: the title character and his wife Madeleine discuss what to do about an ever-growing corpse
an expanding corpse whose feet burst through a door

clues.clue: A Stroll in the Air: Berenger shows off his ability to fly; inspired by Ionesco's childhood floating experience in a whitewashed village
quotes: a character created by this author shows off his ability to fly
this author had the supernatural experience of floating while walking through a whitewashed village

clues.clue: Jack, or Obedience / The Future is in Eggs: Jack rejects girl with two noses; marries Roberta; ends with egg-hatching
quotes: rejects a girl on account of her two noses, but ultimately agrees to marry Roberta
one character ends that play frantically hatching eggs

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Ionesco is a Romanian-French absurdist playwright. His recurring character Berenger appears in Rhinoceros, The Killer, Exit the King, and A Stroll in the Air. Kenneth Tynan's negative review sparked the 'London Controversy' in 1958. Ionesco was a member of the College de Pataphysique. He wrote critical essays in Nu.

clues.clue: Romanian-French absurdist playwright of The Bald Soprano and Rhinoceros
quotes: this absurdist Romanian-French playwright of The Bald Soprano and Rhinoceros
this Romanian Absurdist playwright

clues.clue: Recurring character Berenger appears in Rhinoceros, The Killer, Exit the King, A Stroll in the Air
quotes: Berenger is a recurring character for this creator
this author of the Berenger plays

clues.clue: Kenneth Tynan's 1958 negative review sparked the 'London Controversy'
quotes: In 1958, Kenneth Tynan touched off the so-called 'London Controversy' by writing a brutally negative review of this author's plays.

clues.clue: Member of the College de Pataphysique alongside Duchamp and the Marx Brothers
quotes: The College de Pataphysique's members included the Marx Brothers, Marcel Duchamp, and this Romanian playwright.

cards.front: Play: Jean's skin turns green as he transforms; the typist Daisy leaves the protagonist; a logician proves a dog is a cat using a syllogism
Play: satirizes French behavior under German occupation; the green skin of the title creatures represents Iron Guard and Orpo uniforms
Play: an 'anti-play' in which everything in the opening is described as 'English' seventeen times; the Fire Chief settles the 'doorbell debate'
Play: the maid Mary claims her real name is Sherlock Holmes and recites a poem about objects catching fire; Bobby Watson's entire family shares his name
Play: inspired by the playwright's experience reading an English language primer by Assimil; originally directed by Nicholas Bataille; permanently staged at Theatre de la Huchette
Play: the deaf-mute Orator writes 'ANGELFOOD' on a chalkboard; an invisible Emperor arrives; the Old Man and Old Woman throw themselves out of a window
Play: the Old Man introduces his wife as Semiramis and addresses 'all that is left from humanity' as 'leftovers' that 'can make a very good soup'
Play: a one-act in which the Pupil names all four seasons and can multiply but not subtract; the Professor stabs her, restarting the cycle; the maid warns 'Philology leads to calamity!'
Play: a 400-year-old king confronting death; his wife tells him 'You're going to die at the end of the show'; credited with inventing the tractor and writing all of Shakespeare's plays
Play: a murderer in the 'radiant city' lures victims with a 'picture of a colonel'; the killer does nothing but chuckle and shrug
Play: an ever-growing corpse in a living room; based on the short story 'Oriflamme'; the corpse's feet burst through a door
Author: Kenneth Tynan's 1958 negative review of this playwright's works sparked the 'London Controversy'

summary: Eugene Ionesco (Eugen Ionescu) is a Romanian-French absurdist playwright best known for The Bald Soprano, Rhinoceros, The Chairs, The Lesson, and Exit the King.
